data.label: Seed a synthetic NAS
data.description: Writes a small, deterministic file tree into /tmp/mekhan-crawl-demo (cleaning any prior run first) so the rest of the workflow has something real to crawl WITHOUT touching a production file server. ~7 files across two subdirectories (datasets/, plots/, logs/). Outputs the root path and the file count.

data.label: Crawl the file tree (file-ops crawl)
data.description: The file-ops `crawl` operation: a recursive, streaming, checkpointable directory walk under the `mekhan-crawl-demo/` prefix of the local `/tmp` storage root (i.e. /tmp/mekhan-crawl-demo, the dir seed_nas just wrote). Unlike `list` (which buffers the whole Vec inline), crawl drives the OpenDAL streaming lister and `stat()`s each entry for size+mtime, emitting fixed-size `{path,size,mtime}` batches over the `crawl` control channel (batch_size 3 here, so the ~7 files arrive across several batches). It reads no file bytes — metadata only; integrity hashing is the later step's job. The scalar outputs (count/last_path/batches) summarize the walk; the per-file rows ride the channel for the consumer to fold.

data.label: Fold the crawl batches
data.description: Consumes the crawl step's `crawl` control channel with `join: gather`, so the compiler's gather barrier re-orders the emitted batches by index and parks the whole collection as `{output: [<batch>, …]}` — the inbound `input` token. Each batch is a `{items: [{path,size,mtime}, …]}` dict; this step flattens them into one `{path, size}` list and counts the files. This is the consumer-side fold: the producer just emitted a uniform stream, the join discipline lives on the edge.

data.label: Index by observation (inventory only)
data.description: The hashless OBSERVE half of the lifecycle. POSTs the folded listing to mekhan's `/api/v1/inventory/index` — one `{path}` row per crawled file under the Start `file_server_id`, status `indexed`. This writes `file_inventory` rows ONLY: an indexed file has a location but no claimed content identity yet (crawl read no bytes). It deliberately CANNOT write a catalogue row — claiming an identity is what `register` is for. The rows appear in the app's Inventory view as `indexed`, awaiting a probe+register. The literal `fold.files`, `start.file_server_id`, and `start.mekhan_url` reads in the source MUST stay verbatim so the compiler synthesizes their read-arcs.

data.label: Probe + register (fills both)
data.description: The coupling half. The runner is co-located with the files, so it HASHES each indexed file (stdlib hashlib SHA-256 over the storage root + crawled path) — the migration model where the edge probes and the server couples. It then POSTs one `{file_server_id, path, content_hash, status: "registered", size_bytes, name}` row per file to `/api/v1/inventory/register`, which fills BOTH halves of the equation atomically: a content-addressed `catalogue_entries` row keyed on the hash AND the physical `file_inventory` copy (promoting the same `(file_server_id, path)` row from `indexed` to `registered`). Because every file now carries a hash, `catalogue_inserted` matches the file count — you can no longer register only one side. The literal `fold.files`, `seed.root`, `start.file_server_id`, and `start.mekhan_url` reads in the source MUST stay verbatim so the compiler synthesizes their read-arcs.
